findings.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

sources.quote: it will sell them a video for $34.99 yes it is slightly cheaper than the $40 I mentioned earlier but this is to warm them up
once they purchase this $34.99 video then you can consider them warmed up and then whenever you sell them a piece of content after that it is going to be at least 40 and upwards
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-side pricing ladder or escalation logic exists (§7 GAP). The external chat team sets its own approach; Palm has no documented position on warm-up pricing or escalation sequencing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a two-phase pricing ladder for the external chat team and future in-house chatters: Phase 1 (new sub warm-up) = first video at ~$35; Phase 2 (warmed fan) = $40+ escalating. Write this into the chat team brief and eventually into Palm's in-house chatter SOP (§8 build-toward).

findings.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement

variants.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.

variants.claim: The PPV caption is the last decision point before purchase; embedding specific, timestamped curiosity hooks (rather than generic hype) dramatically increases unlock rates by making the sub feel something personalized and unmissable is inside.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters/copywriters craft captions ending with one or more of these proven curiosity hooks: (1) Timestamped surprise — 'I can't believe I did that at 2:41' (specificity signals authenticity); (2) Personalized connection — 'I want to see if you can spot which part I was thinking about you'; (3) Challenge/gamification — 'There's no way you'll last past what I did at minute 1:21'; (4) Embarrassment/vulnerability — 'I'm pretty embarrassed I did that towards the end, but oh well'; (5) Physical detail — 'I'm still sore from that last part'; (6) Multi-content choice — 'Which one of those positions was your favorite?' These are used as the final sentence only; the rest of the caption continues the sexting/emotional build-up from the conversation. Specificity (exact timestamps, specific details) is emphasised as what makes hooks feel genuine vs scripted.

sources.quote: send a personalized opener that is unique to the person who is receiving it
it must contain one question but no more than one question
I wonder if you'll see the surprise I threw in there for you
I'm still sore from that last part
when you're specific, it seems genuine

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8); there is no internal SOP or opener framework at Palm. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, mandate personalized username-based openers as the baseline standard. Create a training doc that (1) defines what counts as a valid personalized opener, (2) lists banned copy-paste patterns, and (3) drills the one-question rule. Include this in pre-training screening — candidates who default to 'hey how are you' should be filtered.

findings.topic: Chatting ratio KPI and chatter hire threshold
findings.title: Target 1:10 sub-to-chat revenue ratio; hire chatters only after reaching $2–5K/month agency revenue

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: The ratio of subscription revenue to messaging/tip revenue is the single most diagnostic KPI for chatting quality — a 1:3 ratio signals poor chatting; a 1:6 ratio signals elite chatting on the same traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Agency uses the sub:messaging ratio as their primary audit metric when taking on new accounts. Baseline poor performance example: $8K subs, $23K messaging = 1:2.9 ratio. After installing their systems: $8K subs, $53K messaging = 1:6 ratio — same subscription base, same traffic, no new content. They claim the ideal target is 1:4 to 1:6. They present a concrete case study: account went from $31K/month to $62K/month purely from chatting system changes, adding $30K/month net profit. They also frame it as a loss quantification tool: a creator doing $10K/month with a 1:1 ratio is 'leaving $10K+ on the table monthly.'

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters
it should be a 1 to 4 or 1 to 6 ratio. Ideally the pay should be $4,000 from subscriptions, 16 to $24,000 in messages and tips
the ratio for messenger subscription is 5.98 times basically a 1:6 ratio… the total net earnings went from 31,000 in February to 62,000
add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal KPI, ratio target, or hire-threshold documented. This is directly relevant to Palm's ~1yr roadmap to bring chatting in-house and the active AI chatting project. The 1:10 ratio is a concrete benchmark Palm should adopt as a performance target for the external team today and the in-house team when built.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately: share the 1:10 sub-to-chat ratio benchmark with the external chat team as a performance SLA and track it in the existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting infrastructure (§9). Build-toward: use the $3–$5/hour + commission pay structure and Discord management model as the template for in-house chatter hiring. Add 'chatting ratio' as a tracked metric in the Airtable reporting layer.

findings.title: Run a multi-platform traffic mix; no single source carries it

variants.claim: Reliable revenue requires combining several traffic sources rather than relying on one, with each platform graded on traffic quality, not raw views.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends Instagram (primary, highest-quality subs), Threads, and X together, with TikTok optional; X drives high volume but lower-quality subs; explicitly moves away from pure rage-bait because '28.5M views' does not equal money — quality and niche of traffic matter for conversion.

variants.claim: Running several agency-controlled accounts for distribution — rather than relying on one creator-owned account — makes traffic generation independent of creator talent or following.
variants.what_they_do: Jason's BFAM system explicitly involves running 'several accounts' for each creator and managing all posting and distribution internally. This is a quantity-over-quality traffic approach: spread across multiple accounts/platforms so that even if individual accounts underperform, aggregate traffic to the OF page is sufficient to hit $10K+. The creator does not manage or post to these accounts.

sources.quote: you need to be using a combination of all different traffic sources
it doesn't always mean views equals money
we run several accounts and manage all the posting and distribution of the marketing content.
This was completely agency dependent traffic.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-Amin posting (§5 PARTIAL) with traffic strategy across channels undocumented (§5 gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a documented multi-channel traffic mix per creator with per-channel quality grading, so channel investment is steered by conversion quality not view counts.

findings.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Agencies that take creators from zero earn the right to keep a higher commission percentage because the agency is creating all the value; working with already-successful creators compresses margins.
variants.what_they_do: Jason ties commission rate directly to how much value the agency is creating relative to the creator. If the creator brings zero earning history, zero social following, and low talent, the agency justifies a higher-than-standard split because it is building everything from scratch. Conversely, signing a talented creator or one with existing revenue means accepting a compressed margin deal. He treats margin as a direct output of value-creation ratio.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

as a result of our immense value creation, when working with creators, scaling them from absolute zero, we get to keep a higher percent than usual simply due to the fact that we are creating such an immense amount of value in the process.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

findings.title: Rapport, transition, then sell

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.

sources.quote: Step One is General conversation building report... step two is transitioning to a more sexual conversation
conserving your energy you should never be throwing all of your energy away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced; Palm does not own script flow (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward for the in-house and AI chat roadmap. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a mandated rapport-then-transition-then-sell chat framework as the backbone script when chatting comes in-house or is automated.

findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

findings.topic: Reddit LTV vs. other traffic sources
findings.title: Reddit organic NSFW traffic produces the highest fan LTV of any channel — $8–$35 on free pages

variants.claim: Fans acquired through niche-matched NSFW Reddit posts have significantly higher LTV than Instagram or dating-app traffic because they self-selected into a subreddit specifically for their fetish and actively chose to view the content, indicating high purchase intent.
variants.what_they_do: Agency benchmarks LTV by traffic source. Reddit organic NSFW: $8–$35 LTV on free pages. Dating app traffic also historically high LTV. Instagram traffic lower. Agency prioritises Reddit despite declining ROI trend because subscriber quality (intentionality) remains superior. At peak (3 years ago) one viral SFW post could drive 3,000–5,000 subs/day; currently realistic target is 10+ paid subs/day per account on a well-matched creator.

variants.claim: Reddit traffic must be run manually with niche-matched content and captions per subreddit because, unlike IG/X, the poster is effectively the algorithm choosing the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media keeps 100 manual Reddit VAs (each issued a physical phone within four weeks) matching niche content and captioning to specific subreddits while fully automating IG/X/Threads; Reddit free subs cost $0.25-$1 and return $4-$8.

sources.quote: we do see a way way higher LTV from these fans off of Reddit, off of the regular Reddit strategy than we see from Instagram fans
I think it's safe to say that that's the best way to get the highest um highest quality of subscriber to the account
On Reddit, you are the algorithm essentially. You are determining which subreddits
cost per acquisition of a Reddit subscriber is anywhere from 25 cents to a dollar... getting anywhere from $4 to $8 a return

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic-source LTV benchmarking (§9 GAP — no written retention/churn strategy). Palm's whale-hunting dashboard (§9) tracks per-fan spend but does not appear to attribute fans to acquisition channel. This LTV signal is highly actionable for Palm's channel prioritisation decisions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add acquisition-channel tagging to Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so LTV can be broken down by source (IG reel, Reddit, etc.). Even before launching Reddit, build the measurement infra so that if/when Reddit is tested, LTV comparison data is captured from day one. Use the $8–$35 Reddit LTV benchmark as the hurdle rate when evaluating Reddit's cost-per-acquisition against account farming costs.

findings.title: Four-stage whale spend cycle: framework → emotional ladder → payment diversification → drama reset

variants.claim: Whales stop spending because they were never given a psychological framework for why they spend, so agencies must run every whale through a structured four-stage cycle to sustain long-term revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — Framework: tell the whale he is a 'provider' and that providing unlocks relationship progression; reinforce with praise each time he spends ('I love how you provide for me'). Stage 2 — Emotional investment ladder: every ~10 days escalate one non-monetary micro-commitment (write me a poem → use my photo as phone background → buy me a gift → create a Spotify playlist for us), so he thinks about the model constantly without chatters texting 24/7. Stage 3 — Payment diversification: once PPV novelty fades (~3 months), pivot spend to customs, fake bills (electricity, Wi-Fi, fines), personalized gifts he tips for (agency buys cheap version and keeps margin). Stage 4 — Manufactured drama: agency-initiated conflict ('You didn't ask how I was, I was crying all day') that he can only exit by purchasing something; keeps him from going on the offensive and conditions him that misbehaving costs money.

sources.quote: the mistake is not like that you sold too much. It's that you didn't give him the understanding
every 10 days, you want to get one step further down the ladder. So first step is okay, write me a little poem
bills electricity bills Wi-Fi bills police fines giving her personalized gifts that he tips for
we always want to like be at pulp. We want to start the drama, not him

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for this cycle (§8, outsourced). The Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting dashboard (§9) exist but have no corresponding spend-cycle SOP. This is high-priority build-toward material.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map all four stages into a chatter SOP document keyed to the Fan Tracker. Add a 'whale stage' field (1–4) to the Fan Tracker Airtable so supervisors can see where each flagged whale sits in the cycle. Share with the external chat team immediately; embed into the AI chatting project as a core monetization loop.

findings.topic: Employee onboarding and task delegation framework
findings.title: Five-step delegation system: do it yourself → document → idiot-proof → work alongside → KPI-gate

variants.claim: Agencies fail to delegate because they skip directly to handing off tasks; the correct sequence is to personally do the task on Loom first, then optimize, then simplify to yes/no checklist questions, then shadow the employee daily until the KPI is hit consistently.
variants.what_they_do: Step 1: Owner does the task themselves while recording a Loom with live commentary explaining every decision. Step 2: Identify friction points discovered while doing it. Step 3: Reduce all subjective criteria to yes/no binary questions (e.g. 'Does the background look like she actually lives there? Yes/No'; 'Did you see this location in the last 10 reels? Yes/No'). Step 4: Work alongside the employee with daily 15–30 min screen-share standups until they hit the KPI independently. Step 5: Define one clear KPI for the role (e.g. 'minimum 100 free Reddit subs per day') — if they hit it, method doesn't matter; if they miss it, run a three-question diagnostic: Did you follow the SOP? Did you ask for help? If both no → strike system (3 strikes = termination or 30% salary cut). For new hires, drip SOPs week-by-week rather than dumping all documentation at once. Offer a bonus for employees who return a document of every question the SOP failed to answer.

sources.quote: we want to really like do the task ourselves and document it so for example in the agency I always just start a loom video
the minimum it's basically the 100 free subs. With the system 100 free subs
did you follow the SOPs and the sheets and the instructions word by word? If the answer is yes, then it's not his fault
you can break out most task into very simple questions like even chatting

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented delegation or employee-onboarding framework (§10, THIN). The content pipeline has an 8-step SOP (§1) but no structured handoff process for training editors or managers to own it. This framework applies directly to onboarding editors, the Reddit/posting VA (Amin), and future in-house chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this five-step framework as Palm's standard delegation SOP. Apply it immediately to: (1) the editor workflow in §1 — record Loom walkthroughs of the day-counter queue, (2) any Reddit/traffic VAs Palm adds (§5 gap), (3) the first in-house chatter hire. Add a 'role KPI' field to Clerk/Airtable role definitions. Build a simple strike-tracking table in Airtable for team accountability.

findings.title: Automate contract → onboarding form → calendar booking via Zapier

variants.claim: The moment a client fills out a sales intake form, a fully automated chain fires: contract sent via e-signatures.io, welcome email triggered on signing, onboarding form delivered, and kickoff calendar link sent — all without manual intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Three sequential Zapier workflows: (1) Notion sales intake form submission → e-signatures.io contract creation with placeholder fields auto-populated; (2) contract signed → Gmail welcome email with onboarding form link; (3) onboarding form submitted → Gmail email with GoHighLevel calendar booking link. Appointment reminders sent at 24hr, 1hr, and 5min via GHL automation. All built in Notion with a Zapier Pro account ($29.99/mo) and e-signatures.io pay-per-use ($0.49/contract).

sources.quote: e signatures.io, it's not a $30 a month type of subscription to send my contracts. It is you pay per contract sent
sales intake form to contract. So, that's what we want to do
contract signed. Send onboarding form. Perfect. I'm going to go back to my folder

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form wizard onboarding flow for creators (§6) and auto-generated PDF contracts via Puppeteer, but no documented automated chain that sequences contract → onboarding form → kickoff booking without manual steps. The creator-side onboarding is thin; there is no client/brand-side onboarding OS equivalent documented in Palm's baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build the same three-Zap chain for Palm's creator onboarding: sales intake form submission (Airtable or a form) triggers contract PDF (already Puppeteer-built), signing triggers an onboarding packet with a kickoff Calendly/GHL link, and form completion triggers a Telegram or email alert to Josh. Low incremental cost given existing Puppeteer contract system.

findings.title: Run a structured Canva onboarding presentation to kill scope creep at kickoff

variants.claim: A formal onboarding call presentation — covering process steps, revision limits, invoice terms, cancellation policy, and 'how to win' — eliminates scope creep and buyer remorse by setting hard expectations before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Build a Canva slide deck covering: welcome/team intro, reiterate buying decision with case studies, step-by-step delivery process with timelines, revision policy (2 revisions, batch feedback in one email), invoicing terms, meeting cancellation policy (72hr notice), additional services pricing, and monthly strategy call cadence. Present this at the kickoff call before any strategy discussion. Use it as a live walk-through shared on screen. Embed the deck in the client's Notion client OS for future reference.

sources.quote: any frustration is just an unmet expectation. And if we're not doing a good job at setting expectations, then the client's going to be frustrated
I like to use Canva to build this out. So, if I'm on the client call... we're just going to go over a quick onboarding presentation just to make sure we're really aligned on expectations
revisions. If you do have revisions, super happy to help. We limit those to two

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented onboarding call SOP or expectation-setting presentation for creators (§6 is thin). The creator onboarding form wizard collects info but there is no structured kickoff call protocol. This gap means scope creep and misaligned expectations are likely at creator onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm creator onboarding deck covering: what Palm does (social + content pipeline), creator responsibilities (filming, approvals), commission split and invoice cadence, content approval process and turnaround times, and escalation path. Embed in the creator-facing section of the admin portal or deliver via a recorded Loom. Assign to Josh or a future creator success role.

findings.topic: Notion-based fulfillment OS with per-role task board views
findings.title: Build role-filtered Notion task boards so each team member sees only their queue

variants.claim: A single Notion database for all fulfillment tasks, filtered into separate board views per role (videographer, editor, social media manager, CSM, strategist), eliminates cross-team confusion and missed deadlines without requiring multiple databases.
variants.what_they_do: One 'actions' Notion database with status stages (ready to script → scripting → ready to shoot → ready to edit → editing → ready to review → ready to send to client → sent to client → client approved → ready to post → done). Project templates pre-populate task lists (e.g., 8 videos + 3 ads for flagship offer). Each role gets a filtered view showing only their relevant statuses: videographer sees shoot-date calendar; editor sees editing dates and a countdown timer; CSM sees 'ready for review' and 'ready to send to client'; social media manager sees post-date calendar. Internal comments replace Slack/iMessage for revision feedback.

sources.quote: we're going to assign owners at each stages so that it's really really clear
we created a board for that. So, we're going to go to video editor board and boom, ready to edit
the comment. I'm not communicating via Slack. I'm not communicating via iMessage. It's right here.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step content pipeline (§1) with Assets and Tasks tables in Airtable, an editor workflow with day-counter task queue, and 2-slot-per-day scheduling. This is structurally similar. Key gap: Palm's documented editor workflow covers submission→library→grid planner but does not describe per-role filtered views or cross-role handoff statuses within a single task record. The Notion approach adds a unified card that travels through all roles, keeping script, shoot footage, and edit all on one record.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate whether Palm's Airtable Tasks table can be extended with status stages and per-role filtered views (editor view, social media manager view, creator view) rather than rebuilding in Notion. The specific add: attach raw footage links, captions, and revision comments directly to the task card so editors and Amin don't need Slack/Telegram for task-level communication. This is a concrete improvement to the existing mature system.

findings.topic: Monthly NPS survey system with action protocol
findings.title: Send a monthly NPS form to every active client and require a response to every score

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

sources.quote: NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9) for fan retention, but no documented NPS or satisfaction survey system for creator retention. The §9 gap notes no written retention/churn/win-back strategy. This tactic maps directly to that gap — applied to Palm's creators rather than brand clients.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a monthly creator satisfaction pulse (can be a simple Airtable form or Typeform): 1–10 score, one open question ('what could we do better?'), and one win-capture question ('any recent milestone we should celebrate?'). Route responses to Airtable, auto-alert Josh on any score below 7, and have a 48hr response SOP. This costs near-zero and directly addresses the §9 retention gap.

findings.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
findings.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

findings.topic: Role onboarding with daily winning formula checklist
findings.title: Give every new hire a 'daily winning formula' — a repeating Notion task with linked SOPs for each duty

variants.claim: A repeating daily task assigned to each team role, listing every responsibility with embedded Loom SOPs or email templates, eliminates guesswork, prevents the employee from inventing their own process, and lets the founder delegate without micromanaging.
variants.what_they_do: For each role (editor, CSM, appointment setter, etc.): create a Notion task template called '[Role] Daily Winning Formula' that repeats every weekday. Each checklist item links to an SOP (Loom video transcript pasted into Notion) or has a toggle with an exact email template. Example CSM items: 'Maintain daily updates in Notion by 10am', 'Review and QC all video content within 4 hours of submission', 'Send content to clients for approval', 'Update client portal'. Task auto-populates each morning. Pair with a 30-day onboarding course (gallery of Loom recordings) and pre-day-1 through day-6 onboarding checklist.

sources.quote: daily winning formula is basically what we'll do with this... I make it a repeating task every weekday... this is a task that just populates every single day for the client success manager
the reason your team would suck is because you're not actually equipping them with things like this to actually do well
every time I found myself doing something repeatedly, I basically just documented it, systematized it using Notion, and then trained someone else to handle it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Clerk roles (editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) and an editor workflow with a day-counter task queue (§1, §10), but no documented daily winning formula, role-specific onboarding checklist, or SOP library linked to recurring tasks. The §10 gap notes no hiring criteria or scaling plan. This tactic is immediately applicable to Palm's editors and Amin (contractor posting role).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's two most active roles — editor and Amin (manual posting) — create a daily Airtable or Notion checklist with 5–8 items each, linked to existing SOPs (or quick Loom recordings). Specifically for the editor: items like 'check task queue, pull next 2 slots, upload to Cloudflare, submit for approval.' For Amin: 'check Telegram topic, post per schedule, reply /posted.' This is a low-effort, high-leverage move to reduce dependency on Josh/Evan for day-to-day direction.

findings.topic: Loom-to-SOP pipeline for building a scalable SOP library
findings.title: Record a Loom, paste the auto-transcript into ChatGPT, and get a step-by-step SOP in minutes

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.
variants.what_they_do: Loom.com recording → copy transcript → ChatGPT prompt → formatted SOP pasted into Notion SOP library, tagged by department (sales, client success, hiring, organic marketing, etc.) and status (draft/active). SOPs are linked from daily winning formula tasks and role onboarding courses via hyperlinks. Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.

sources.quote: what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it
these are all the SOPs. This is sales, client success, hiring, etc.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented SOP library (§10 gap). The content pipeline (§1) is highly systematized in code and CLAUDE.md but not in a human-readable SOP format accessible to editors or Amin. The Loom-to-SOP method is extremely low-friction and matches Palm's current AI-assisted workflow culture.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Start Palm's SOP library with three highest-leverage SOPs: (1) editor submission-to-approval workflow, (2) Amin Telegram posting protocol, (3) creator content filming brief format. Use Loom + ChatGPT as described. Store in Airtable or a Notion page linked from the admin portal. Target: 10 SOPs in 2 weeks, then assign new SOPs as a recurring task whenever a process question is asked more than once.

findings.topic: Meta ads as primary traffic channel
findings.title: Run scripted Meta video ads direct-to-OF to bypass IG algorithm dependency

variants.claim: Purpose-built Meta ad creatives linked directly to OnlyFans via the OF tracking API can deliver 50–100 paid subs per day per creator without relying on organic IG reach.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 bespoke video ad scripts per model (e.g. 'boyfriend films her scrolling OF messages' scenario). Creator films them. Agency runs a $40/day testing phase across all creatives for 7 days, then identifies best cost-per-acquisition winner. Winning creative gets scaled with increased budget. Links go directly to OF using the OnlyFans API tracking link, with Meta pixel attached so both subscriber volume and spend are measurable. No intermediate redirect links needed.

sources.quote: we will give creators ad scripts to make
we'll do $40 per day every single day, and we'll see which one the algorithm actually favors
we literally just use OnlyFans API. We get a tracking link from there, so we can track everything, we can add the pixel in there

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented paid-traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution strategy not documented). Palm's current funnel is organic social → OF. This is a fully orthogonal paid channel that de-risks the IG dependency Palm currently has.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Meta ads playbook: (1) create a script bank of 5–10 ad concepts per creator, (2) run a structured 7-day/$40-per-day creative test, (3) integrate OF API tracking links + Meta pixel for CPA visibility, (4) scale winning creative. Start with one creator as a pilot. This directly addresses the IG instability risk flagged in §1 and §5.

findings.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
findings.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

findings.title: Track 5 profit layers, not just creator gross revenue

variants.claim: Creator gross revenue is the least useful number for judging agency health; founders must track five sequential layers down to owner profit per hour.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.

sources.quote: level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks creator gross via Inflow/FansMetric dashboards and computes agency net for invoicing (§7), but there is no documented gross-profit-per-creator, owner-profit, or owner-profit-per-hour metric. The invoicing system auto-computes splits and weekly PDFs, but stops at agency net — levels 3-5 of Dylan's waterfall are a GAP (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'profit waterfall' view in the admin portal: creator gross → agency net (already computed) → gross profit (subtract per-creator fulfillment costs: chat team allocation, editor time, VA time, software) → owner profit (subtract fixed overhead) → owner profit per founder hour. Surface this weekly alongside the existing invoice dashboard. This is a high-priority instrumentation task given Palm's current THIN baseline on profitability metrics.

findings.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

findings.title: Every team member must own a single measurable number — vague roles produce vague performance

variants.claim: Any metric that no one on the team explicitly owns will default back to the founder, making him the bottleneck and preventing scale.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan assigns a specific owned number to every role: setter owns qualified booked calls; closer owns cash collected; chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality; account manager owns creator retention and content compliance; creative director owns post volume and content performance; ops manager owns scorecard completion and weekly execution rhythm. He uses a 5-level accountability ladder: (1) wait to be told, (2) ask what to do, (3) recommend and act, (4) act then inform, (5) full ownership / exceptions only. Chatter managers should be at level 3-4; ops/sales managers at level 5. The coaching method to move someone from level 2→3: 'Before you come to me with a problem, bring me the problem, three options, and your recommendation.'

sources.quote: Every person on your team should own a number because any number nobody owns defaults back to you
Level three, recommend act. These people, they come to you and say, here's the problem. Here's the options. Here's my recommendation. Do you agree?
the setter owns qualified calls, the closer owns cash collected, the chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has defined Clerk roles (super_admin, admin, editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) but these are access-control roles, not outcome-ownership definitions (§10 GAP). There are no documented KPIs, scorecards, or accountability levels per role. The chat team is outsourced (§8), so the chatter manager role isn't yet internal, but the account manager, editor, and Amin (manual posting) roles have no documented owned numbers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For each internal Palm role, define one owned number and the accountability level it should operate at: editor → content posted on schedule + revision rate (level 3-4); Amin/posting contractor → on-time post compliance rate (level 3); future chat manager → PPV conversion rate + response time (level 4-5). Add these to a simple weekly scorecard reviewed by Josh/Evan. This is foundational infrastructure before Palm scales headcount further (§10 GAP).

findings.topic: Minimum viable operating system / weekly scorecard
findings.title: Run the business on a 4-part minimum viable operating system anchored by a weekly scorecard

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

sources.quote: you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has strong content pipeline instrumentation (§1) and invoicing automation (§7), but no weekly business-level scorecard covering all four domains, no delivery operating system for creator accounts, and no documented founder replacement ladder (§10 GAP, §9 GAP). The SMM hub redesign (§11) addresses social content visibility but not the business-health scorecard layer Dylan describes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'Agency Weekly Scorecard' page in the admin portal (or a recurring Airtable view) covering all four scorecard domains. Prioritize delivery tracking first (content compliance + chat performance + revenue per creator) since that's where Palm currently has the most instrumentation gaps. The founder replacement ladder should be applied immediately to Evan and Josh's recurring tasks — document and delegate at least one recurring task per week. This is a direct complement to Palm's in-flight SMM hub consolidation (§11).

findings.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
findings.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

findings.topic: Chat performance tracking within delivery operating system
findings.title: Track chat performance weekly as a core delivery KPI — don't discover underperformance after revenue drops

variants.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

sources.quote: you spot the chatter that's performance is declining before the month closes out and the revenue drops
A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.
the chatter start to underperform. Someone notices too late cuz the chat manager slacked off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8) and has no internal chat performance SOP. The whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) capture some fan-side data, but there is no weekly chat performance scorecard tied to the delivery review cadence. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap projects (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward in-house chatting, define the chat performance KPIs that the future chatter manager will own: PPV conversion rate, average revenue per fan per week, response time, welcome message performance, and reactivation rate on going-cold fans. Build these into the weekly scorecard from day one of in-house operations rather than retrofitting later. In the interim, request these metrics from the external chat team weekly and add them to Palm's existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting reporting (§9) to start building baseline data now.

findings.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
findings.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

findings.title: Multi-stage application filter: attention check + AP score + template effort scoring

variants.claim: Embedding a small compliance instruction in the job post (e.g. 'write I understand at the bottom') combined with checking onlinejobs.ph Application Points and requiring a filled template eliminates ~40–50% of low-quality applicants before any interview.
variants.what_they_do: Three-layer pre-screen: (1) embed a hidden instruction in the job post requiring a specific phrase to prove they read it; (2) on onlinejobs.ph, prioritize applicants who spent more Application Points (higher AP = higher motivation, lower AP = mass-applying); (3) send all applicants a structured template (name, age, experience, English level, open-ended questions like 'describe your communication style') and filter out anyone who answers lazily or skips fields.

sources.quote: put a simple instruction next to the job post. So, if you apply, text me I understand at the bottom of your application
you always want to look at the AP... if there's an application with just one AP, one application point, means he probably sent it to like a thousand other agencies
they should fill out a template sent in the back to you with like the name, the age, job experience, English level, and just a couple of questions

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring baseline (§8 outsourced). This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build these three filters into Palm's future chatter hiring SOP as Stage 1. The AP-score filter is onlinejobs.ph-specific and low-effort; include it as a default screen. Design the template with at least one open-ended question specifically to measure effort, not content.

findings.topic: Three non-negotiable reel criteria: aesthetics, emotional curve, WTF moment
findings.title: Reject any reel missing aesthetics + emotional curve + pattern-interrupt — reshoot it

variants.claim: The difference between 6K-view and 6M-view reels is an engineered three-part formula: matching aesthetics to creator USP, a scripted look-away→look-in→smile emotional arc, and a deliberate stop-scroll pattern-interrupt moment.
variants.what_they_do: Aesthetics: outfit, location, lighting, framing all matched to the creator's visual USP (e.g., if USP is large chest + black hair, 80% of content angles highlight that — not hidden in hoodies). Emotional curve: reels are scripted with a 3-beat arc — look away (living her life) → look in (she noticed you) → smile (girlfriend moment) — engineered to create GFE in 3 seconds, repeated across the ~46 views a typical fan sees before subscribing. WTF moment: every reel has a deliberate background event (someone walking into a pole, a cat crossing) that breaks autopilot scrolling and triggers rewatches, which signals the algorithm.

sources.quote: Look away says she's living her life. She's not looking at you. Look in says she just noticed you watching. Then smile.
the emotional curve is the precise difference between a clip that gets 6,000 views and a clip that hits 6 million views
every reel has a stop scroll moment…something in the background, something natural, something the viewer didn't expect

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies *what* to film from viral data but does not codify *how* to film it at this granularity. The emotional curve and WTF moment criteria are absent from Palm's current documented production standards. Palm's editor workflow has approval gates but no stated rejection criteria at this level.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add these three criteria as a mandatory editor QA checklist in Palm's Task/approval workflow. For aesthetics: add a 'creator USP card' to each creator's Airtable record (visual USP, don'ts list) that editors reference on every clip review. For emotional curve and WTF moment: create a one-page shoot brief template with examples that goes to creators/videographers before every filming session. Clips failing any criterion should be flagged for reshoot, not edited around.

findings.topic: Vault structuring + custom hotkeys per creator
findings.title: Structure OF vault with labeled categories + creator-specific hotkeys to cut reply time to 50 seconds

variants.claim: A disorganized vault and generic hotkeys are the single largest hidden tax on chatter productivity; restructuring vault with categories and building custom per-creator hotkeys raised reply rates 60% in two weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Vault restructured within week 1: every content piece labeled by category (boy/girl, tier list, JOI, rapport building), exact price per video, and a defined script order (first PPV → second PPV → optional upsell → conditional branches). Chatters open CreatorHero, see the script order, copy-paste, send — 50-second reply time, no scrolling. Hotkeys are custom per creator: matched to the creator's speaking style, age, aesthetic, and specific vault content. Separate hotkeys for small talk, transitions, objection handling. The 18-year-old blonde's hotkeys use youth lingo; the milf's don't. Result: chatters spend 80% less time searching, 80% more time selling.

sources.quote: we label everything. categories, scripts, boy, girl, tier list, jerk off instructions, rapport building, every single pricing for the video, and the script order
we build custom hotkeys per creator, per script, and they are specific to the way she speaks, the way she looks
our reply rate went up by like 60% in the first two weeks just from this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no documented vault structure, hotkey system, or script-order protocol internally. This is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap. Palm's AI chatting project should also encode this structure: a well-categorized vault + conditional script logic maps directly to an AI chat decision tree.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, design vault structure and hotkeys as launch-day requirements, not later optimizations. For each creator: (1) categorize all vault content at onboarding, (2) price-tag every piece, (3) define a numbered script order with conditional branches, (4) build hotkeys matched to that creator's voice/aesthetic stored in CreatorHero or equivalent. For the AI chat project, the script-order logic and conditional branches are the conversation flow architecture — treat the vault structure doc as the training data schema.

findings.topic: PPV and welcome message split testing — optimize for revenue-per-100 not buy rate
findings.title: Split-test PPV price and welcome message on revenue-per-100-subs metric, not open or buy rate

variants.claim: Optimizing for the obvious metric (buy rate, open rate) consistently picks the wrong winner; the correct optimization target is revenue generated per 100 subscribers, which requires testing the full downstream chain.
variants.what_they_do: First PPV: tested $6, $8, $9, $10. Buy rates were 67%, 65%, 59%, 52% respectively. Most agencies would pick $6 (highest buy rate). They picked $8 because per-100-subs revenue: $6×67=$402 vs $8×65=$520 — 30% more revenue. Additionally, second-PPV buy rate was higher at $8 than $6. Welcome message: tested four variants over four weeks. Variant 1 had 85% open rate (highest). Variant 4 had lowest open rate but highest reply rate. They picked variant 4 because reply rate starts a conversation, and conversations generate sales — open rate without reply is worse than not opening at all. The unifying principle: find the metric that is one step closer to actual revenue, not the most visible one.

sources.quote: we tested four prices on a paint account with a free trial. $6, 8, 9, 10…we picked eight because of total revenue
the metric that matters is revenue per 100. How much money do 100 new subscribers generate? That's the number to chase.
We picked variant 4 because it had the lowest view rate but the highest reply rate

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for PPV pricing or welcome message testing at Palm (§7 — GAP on pricing model; §8 — chatting outsourced). This is pure learn-from-others territory. The revenue-per-100-subs framework is a concrete, implementable KPI Palm should adopt for both the current outsourced team and the future in-house build.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish 'revenue per 100 subscribers' as Palm's primary backend performance KPI. For the current outsourced chat team: request this metric in weekly reporting. When building in-house: codify a split-test protocol for first PPV price (test 4 price points, measure over minimum 100 subs) and welcome messages (test 4 variants over 4 weeks). Build both into a standard new-creator launch checklist. Add 'reply rate' as the primary welcome-message metric in Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9), not open rate.

findings.topic: Net profit vs gross revenue focus
findings.title: Set net-profit targets, not gross-revenue milestones

variants.claim: Agencies obsessing over gross revenue figures (e.g. $100K/mo) often keep only 10–40% as net profit, so every strategic decision should be benchmarked against what the operator actually takes home.
variants.what_they_do: Renato coaches clients to frame all goals as net-profit targets (e.g. 'I want to keep $50K/mo') rather than gross figures. He illustrates with a client doing ~$100K gross who keeps $65–70K via a salary-model structure vs. another doing $100K gross who keeps only $30–40K via a rev-share structure — same gross, very different outcomes. He explicitly tells clients to evaluate every agency decision (traffic platform, model structure, scaling direction) by the profit it will produce.

sources.quote: profit is everything. Way too many people get confused in the game about fancy figures like a million a month
OnlyFans takes 20% if you're at a 100k gross revenue, you're left with 80K. His two salary models take less than 3K
Hit net profit targets. I want to keep 50k. I want to keep 100k. Every decision you make in your agency should be going towards this.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline documented for how Palm frames its own financial targets (§7 GAP — no documented pricing/commission model or profit-margin discipline). Palm tracks commission % per creator and auto-computes weekly invoices, but there is no written framework for evaluating decisions by net-profit impact.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's target net-profit margin per creator and per agency overall. Build a simple P&L model (OF cut, chatter cost, editor cost, tech cost, commission split) so every new creator signing or platform decision is evaluated against net contribution, not top-line revenue. Surface this in the admin portal as a per-creator profitability view.

findings.title: Salary-model structure to maximize agency net-profit margin

variants.claim: Paying creators a flat monthly salary ($1K–$3K) instead of a revenue-share split lets the agency keep the vast majority of gross revenue and creates agency-side leverage because the creator depends entirely on the agency for traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Renato's preferred structure: agency owns all traffic/marketing, creator records PPV content and receives a fixed salary (examples given: $1K–$3K/mo). At $100K gross, OF takes 20% ($20K), salaries cost ~$3–5K total for two models, chatting costs ~10% ($10K), leaving $65–70K net profit vs. $30–40K under a typical rev-share deal. He frames this as 'hybrid OFM.' The creator cannot replicate results independently because all marketing is agency-built, so churn leverage flips to the agency.

sources.quote: A very good system that I think is very under looked is salary models.
you pay them 2K, 3K, 1K depends on what deal you made with them. And all of the profit is yours
he's doing hybrid OFM. Let's take his example because it's easier to calculate.

palm_comparison.vs_us: §7 GAP — Palm has no documented commission model or deal-structure rationale. Current model is commission % set per creator at onboarding (rev-share implied). Salary-model structure is not referenced anywhere in Palm's baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate introducing a salary-model tier for creators where Palm controls the primary traffic channels (IG/TikTok accounts, Reddit, etc.). Define criteria for when a salary deal is preferable to rev-share (e.g. Palm owns and has built the social accounts from scratch). Document a deal-structure decision tree in §7 and model the net-profit differential in the invoicing system.

findings.topic: Traffic ownership as agency leverage and churn protection
findings.title: Own the traffic accounts to create model-retention leverage

variants.claim: Agencies that control their own traffic infrastructure (social accounts, follower bases) retain leverage when a creator leaves, because creators cannot replicate the results independently — turning what is normally a creator-holds-all-leverage situation into an agency-side asset.
variants.what_they_do: Renato builds and owns the IG/TikTok (and other platform) accounts himself, not the creator. If a creator leaves, the traffic asset stays with the agency. He contrasts this with 'over-glorified chatting agencies' that merely attach their chatting system to a creator's existing audience — when she leaves, everything is gone. He cites a coaching client whose main model left the day before a call, immediately wiping out ~30–40% of revenue, as evidence of a dangerously unstructured agency.

sources.quote: You have to be in control of the traffic, okay? You have to control the traffic you're bringing in.
if she leaves, this 20K extra that he made — out the window. There's no more 20K extra. And then a model won't leave because she can't replicate this on her own.
First rule of structure, own the traffic. You have to own the traffic. That gives you leverage.

palm_comparison.vs_us: This aligns with Palm's existing AI-persona model (§0/§3) where Palm builds and owns social accounts. For real creators, however, the social presence is typically the creator's own account, meaning Palm does not own the traffic asset in those cases. This is a structural vulnerability Palm shares with the agencies Renato criticizes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For new real-creator signings, explore a hybrid arrangement where Palm builds and administers at least one dedicated traffic account (e.g. a themed or 'theme page' account) on behalf of the creator that Palm retains rights to if the creator departs. Document this as a standard clause in the creator contract (already uses Puppeteer PDF contracts). This converts a traffic dependency into a Palm-owned asset.

findings.title: Sign only one niche of creator to make content systems copy-paste repeatable

variants.claim: Signing creators across wildly different niches forces bespoke strategy for each, killing operational efficiency; specializing in one niche (e.g. goth/cosplay) lets the agency reuse the same content verticals and growth playbook on every new signing.
variants.what_they_do: Renato advises agencies to pick one niche they know how to scale and only sign creators in that niche. Example: client Ricardo primarily signs goth/cosplay models, so every new signing gets the same proven content vertical approach. Contrast with client Alexander who has models in mixed niches and struggles to know what content strategy to apply to each. The principle: once you have a proven system for a niche, every new creator is just another instance of running that system.

sources.quote: systemize everything, okay? Systemize everything and copy-paste the system on other models.
sign only the models you know how to scale, that you're good at. So, for example, only goth niche. Ricardo does this where he mostly signs goth/cosplay models.
If a model comes in, immediately, in the next month you can get an extra 20K for her from scratch, fully.

palm_comparison.vs_us: §6 GAP — Palm has no documented acquisition criteria or niche targeting strategy. Creator signing is Josh-DM-led and opportunistic. There is no stated niche specialization or content-vertical repeatability requirement in the onboarding process.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a niche focus for Palm's creator roster (even informally at first). Document which content verticals and inspo-source accounts in the §1 pipeline correspond to that niche. Add a 'niche fit' qualification criterion to the creator onboarding form so new signings are pre-screened against the niches where Palm already has proven content systems.

findings.title: Diversify traffic across platforms proactively to hedge against IG ban waves

variants.claim: Agencies relying exclusively on Instagram for traffic are highly exposed to ban waves; diversifying to other platforms (non-IG) immediately before or during a ban wave protects revenue and can generate significant incremental net profit within weeks.
variants.what_they_do: When IG bans hit Renato's clients hard, he prescribed a deliberate step-back from Instagram and shifted effort to unspecified 'other platforms' (intentionally not named — paid coaching content). Client Ricardo grew accounts on new platforms from scratch organically (no paid ads), saw minimal traction in week one, then hit 20K extra net profit in the following 10 days using 3–4 accounts totaling ~33K followers. Renato describes the method as 'full AI marketing' targeting USA audiences. The tactic is explicitly framed as platform-risk mitigation, not just growth.

sources.quote: we took a step back from Instagram as Instagram is banning way too many accounts right now
half of his Instagram accounts got banned. Half of your Instagram accounts get banned, you cut your pretty much revenue in half.
he got maybe 100, 200, 300 followers, not too much, but then in the remaining 10 days, when the accounts got a bit of traction, he's right now pretty much made 20K extra

palm_comparison.vs_us: §5 GAP — Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented. Palm uses IG and TikTok (via Publer/Telegram) but has no written multi-platform diversification policy or contingency plan for IG ban waves. §4 notes that real-creator warmup/cadence is not formalized. The IG ban risk is live and present for Palm's real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately document a platform-diversification contingency SOP: if any real creator loses >2 IG accounts in a 30-day window, trigger migration of effort to at least one backup platform. Identify which platforms (Reddit, TikTok, Threads, X, Snapchat) Palm can activate quickly given existing infrastructure. Add a 'backup platform' field to the Creators table in Airtable and assign one per active creator now, before a ban event forces a rushed response.

findings.title: Choose horizontal (more models) vs vertical (deeper per model) scaling based on profit structure

variants.claim: Agencies should consciously choose between scaling horizontally (more creators, same system) or vertically (fewer creators, maximize each) rather than defaulting to signing as many models as possible, because more models often means more headache without proportionally more profit.
