variants.what_they_do: All social accounts avoid the term 'OnlyFans' entirely in captions, bios, and comments. They route followers to OF via a free Linktree link-in-bio, keeping the social account clean of flagged keywords.

sources.quote: never use the word only fans on your social media accounts. They will shadow ban you if you do it and your accounts become worthless

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Beacons as a link-in-bio tool for AI personas (§3). It is not explicitly documented whether real-creator social accounts follow the same no-OF-mention rule. This is a basic hygiene policy that should be formalized for all Palm-managed accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a content policy rule across all Palm-managed social accounts (real and AI): never use 'OnlyFans' in text; always route via Beacons/Linktree. Add this as a checklist item in the editor approval step (§1, step 8) and the creator onboarding form (§6).

findings.topic: OF subscription vs PPV content tiering
findings.title: Tier OF content: subscription vault slightly better than free social; reserve best for PPV chat

variants.claim: Subscription content must be noticeably better than free social content to justify the sub, but must not reveal the creator's full range — that depth is reserved for PPV sold in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: They operate a three-tier content ladder: (1) free social teaser, (2) subscription vault that is 'slightly better' than social but deliberately incomplete, (3) explicit PPV sold during chat. Reels on social are kept to 5–10 seconds for best algorithmic performance. Background quality is emphasized as a trust signal, with American-aesthetic backgrounds used since the primary customer base is US-based.

sources.quote: what is very important is to make sure that what they get in the subscription is slightly better than what they get on the free social media accounts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social content production (§1) but has a documented thin spot on OF vault/PPV content strategy (§2). This competitor's three-tier model gives a concrete framework Palm currently lacks. The social reel length guideline (5–10 seconds) is also not codified in Palm's pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a formal three-tier content policy for each Palm creator: social teaser → subscription vault → PPV/chat exclusive. Add reel duration guidance (5–10 seconds) to the content pipeline brief (§1). Work with the external chat team (§8) to ensure PPV sequencing aligns with this ladder.

findings.title: Escalate PPV content soft-to-hard to maximize purchase frequency per fan

variants.claim: Sending explicit content too early (e.g. the most extreme acts in the first message) destroys repeat-purchase motivation — always escalate gradually from soft to hard to extend the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a soft-to-hard content escalation: early PPV is tame/suggestive; progressively more explicit content is unlocked as the fan continues to purchase. The underlying logic is to keep the fan always feeling there is 'more to unlock,' extending the revenue relationship as long as possible before they disengage.

sources.quote: sell from soft to hard. It's super important

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team with no internal SOP (§8). This soft-to-hard escalation framework is a high-value build-toward tactic for Palm's planned in-house chat team and AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture soft-to-hard escalation as a core principle in the future Palm chatter SOP. Define 3–5 content 'tiers' per creator (e.g. teasing → topless → explicit acts) and script the upsell transition between each tier. Share this framework with the current external chat team as a quality guideline while in-house capability is built.

findings.title: Use mass DMs only to open conversations, never to push offers directly

variants.claim: Mass DMs sent as direct sales offers perform poorly; they work best exclusively as conversation-starters, which then lead to individualized upsells that earn far more.
variants.what_they_do: Mass DMs are used solely to initiate engagement (e.g. a simple personal-feeling opener). Once a fan responds and a conversation is live, the chatter pivots to individualized PPV sales. They explicitly avoid blasting offers via mass DM, framing it as '10x more money' to sell through conversation vs. cold offers. They also advise reading whether the fan is in a context where he can engage (not at dinner, not busy) before pushing a sale.

sources.quote: Mass DMs is a really important tool to get a conversation started and this should be the only thing that you want to use it for

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8). This mass-DM-as-opener-only rule is a concrete, testable tactic that Palm's in-house/AI chat roadmap should adopt. It also has implications for how Palm might use OF's mass-message feature for real creators today — worth validating with the current external team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the current external chat team on this principle and confirm their mass-DM usage aligns (opener only, not offer blast). Encode 'mass DM = conversation opener only' as a standing rule in the future Palm chatter playbook and AI chat system prompts.

findings.title: Record all posting and chatting actions from day one to generate optimization data

variants.claim: Failing to log what you post and what you do in chat means you cannot identify what is actually working, wasting time and causing you to repeat mistakes indefinitely.
variants.what_they_do: They track all posting activity and chat interactions in spreadsheets (Excel templates), maintaining a consistent posting schedule for at least one month without changes before evaluating results. They also follow other creators' accounts deliberately to train the algorithm to surface their content to relevant audiences.

sources.quote: Record everything you do. Now, this gives you valuable feedback on how to proceed in the future. I made the mistake by not doing that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature content pipeline with Airtable tracking for social posts (§1) and whale-hunting/fan CRM data collection underway (§9). However, a real-creator posting cadence/frequency standard is flagged as a GAP (§1), and no chat-performance logging exists internally (§8 outsourced). The competitor's discipline of holding a schedule constant for ≥1 month before changing it is a useful operational norm Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a policy to Palm's content pipeline: once a posting schedule is set for a creator, hold it unchanged for a minimum of 4 weeks before any cadence adjustment, and document the rationale for any change. For the chat roadmap, design logging into the chatter workflow from day one — even simple metrics (messages sent, PPV offers made, conversion rate) will be invaluable for optimization.

findings.topic: AI-generated marketing content to reduce creator dependency
findings.title: Use AI-generated social/marketing content to replace or supplement creator-produced content

variants.claim: AI-generated marketing content removes the need for the creator to be a skilled content producer, making the agency's output independent of creator talent.
variants.what_they_do: Jason's agency uses AI to generate a significant portion of its marketing content (top-of-funnel social content). This is distinct from OF vault/PPV content — it is the promotional material used to drive traffic. By generating this content internally with AI, the agency no longer requires the creator to film high-quality social content consistently, reducing friction and creator-side dependencies.

sources.quote: we also use AI to generate a lot of the marketing content that we use, which again removes a lot of the friction from the model side in needing to be talented, in needing to execute at a high level, in needing to be on time.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already produces AI social content for some real creators and runs full AI personas (§0, §3). Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and agency-run, which partially achieves the same friction-reduction. However, using AI to generate marketing content for real creator accounts (rather than just AI persona accounts) is not formally documented as a tactic. This is a meaningful overlap area — Palm is closer to this than most agencies.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally document and expand the use of AI-generated marketing assets for real creator accounts as part of the BFAM-equivalent workflow. Specifically: identify which content types for real creators can be AI-generated (text posts, captions, carousel graphics, voiceover reels) vs. must-be-creator-filmed (face content). Build this into the content pipeline (§1) as a content-type routing decision.

findings.title: Retain creators by delivering fast zero-to-revenue results with minimal creator effort required

variants.claim: Creators stay and remain satisfied when the agency takes them from $0 to thousands per month with only a few hours of work per week on their side.
variants.what_they_do: Jason explicitly frames low creator effort + fast revenue as the core creator retention mechanism. By removing friction (creators only upload to a drive, agency does everything else), creators work just a few hours per week. Combined with real revenue results fast (e.g., $12K month one), creator churn is low because the value proposition is undeniable. He positions this as why he can 'keep creators incredibly happy.'

sources.quote: we keep our creators incredibly happy because we take them from earning zero dollars to multiple thousands of dollars every single month with barely any input from their side, working just a few hours per week.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention or churn strategy (§9 🔲 GAP). Palm's system does reduce creator friction (§1 pipeline, agency-run posting), but there is no written framework for measuring creator satisfaction, tracking churn signals, or running a success cadence with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's creator retention playbook: (1) set explicit effort expectations at onboarding ('you work X hours/week, we do the rest'); (2) establish a 30/60/90 day revenue milestone check-in cadence; (3) track creator churn signals in Airtable (missed uploads, reduced responsiveness, revenue plateau complaints). Use fast early results as the primary retention lever and document the expected revenue ramp per creator tier.

findings.topic: Competitor-audience targeting with quality filtering before bot interactions
findings.title: Filter competitor follower lists to remove bots/inactives before outreach

variants.claim: Maximizing bot ROI requires pre-filtering scraped follower lists to remove accounts with no profile picture, bot signals, or recent creation dates before any interaction.
variants.what_they_do: Before running any bot interaction, they scrape a target competitor's followers, then apply filters: remove no-profile-picture accounts, remove recently created accounts, remove known-bot signals. Only the remaining 'qualified' pool receives story likes, post likes, or DMs. This is framed as maximizing ROI per interaction rather than spraying randomly.

sources.quote: out of the hundred thousand people some of them will be inactive some of them will be Bots some of them will not have a profile picture and maybe just created the account and so on we can literally filter those out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outbound traffic system at all (§5 GAP). This filtering logic is a best-practice layer on top of any mass-interaction strategy — Palm should adopt it if/when implementing any bot or mass-outreach traffic layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm implements any mass-IG-outreach tool, build a pre-flight filtering step into the SOP: (1) pull follower list via scraper, (2) filter out no-avatar accounts, (3) filter accounts created <90 days ago, (4) optionally filter accounts with zero posts. Codify these as default filter presets in whatever tooling is chosen.

findings.topic: Comment-like bot for multi-person reach from single interaction
findings.title: Post-comment likes reach ~100 viewers per action vs. 1 for story likes

variants.claim: Automatically liking posts' comments reaches roughly 100 people per action (others viewing the comment thread) versus story likes which reach only the single account holder.
variants.what_they_do: Same audience-sourcing and filtering pipeline as the story-like bot, but the action is liking a post comment rather than a story. The upside: the comment notification and the model's bot-account profile are visible to all other users engaging with that comment thread — estimated ~100 viewers per single like action — multiplying reach per interaction unit.

sources.quote: with the comments compared to the story views is with the story if you view somebody's story or if you even like it that's one action and it can only reach one person... with a comment I mean it's unlimited how many people can see it but typically about 100 people or so

palm_comparison.vs_us: No equivalent at Palm (§5 GAP). This is a force-multiplier variant of IG engagement automation that Palm could layer in alongside story-like automation for higher reach efficiency.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing IG bot traffic (see story-like finding above), run a parallel A/B: story-like bot vs. comment-like bot on the same filtered audience pool. Measure downstream: profile visits, follows, link-in-bio clicks, OF subs. Use the higher-performing format as primary. Comment likes may be particularly effective on high-engagement posts in male-interest niches.

findings.topic: Credential isolation — bot accounts never use model's main IG login
findings.title: All bot activity runs on secondary accounts; model's main IG credentials never shared

variants.claim: Keeping bot operations entirely on secondary/shadow accounts means the model's primary IG is never at risk of a ban even if bot accounts get flagged.
variants.what_they_do: Across all four bot types (story likes, comment likes, mass DM, repost), the agency explicitly never takes the model's actual IG login or password. All interactions originate from purpose-built secondary accounts operating in the background. This is repeated as a selling point three separate times in the video.

sources.quote: we would never need the password of the login detail of your Instagram so it would never actually have to use your model's Instagram account so it will never get banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already applies a strong credential-isolation and hardware-isolation principle to its AI persona accounts (§3/§4 — GrapheneOS Pixel, 1 SIM/persona, vault-stored creds). However, this practice is not documented for real-creator traffic operations (§5 GAP). The underlying principle is the same; Palm should extend it explicitly to any real-creator outbound bot layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm adopts any IG bot/automation traffic layer for real creators, codify credential isolation as a non-negotiable rule in the platform-risk SOP: model's primary IG login is NEVER entered into any third-party automation tool. All bot activity uses dedicated secondary accounts with separate Gmail/SIM per account, consistent with Palm's existing AI-account hygiene practices (§3).

findings.topic: Automated cross-platform repost — TikTok/YouTube Shorts to IG, watermark-free
findings.title: Repost bot auto-downloads TikTok/YT Shorts and re-uploads to IG with no watermark

variants.claim: An automated repost bot removes the TikTok watermark and re-uploads the video to Instagram Reels, expanding reach to a second platform with zero manual effort.
variants.what_they_do: When the model posts to TikTok or YouTube Shorts, the bot detects the new video, downloads it (stripping the TikTok watermark automatically), and re-uploads it to Instagram. No login credentials required from the model. This runs fully in the background — set-and-forget. The stated benefit is reaching both the TikTok and IG audiences from a single piece of content.

sources.quote: when your model goes ahead and posts a Tick Tock or even a YouTube short this bot will quite literally download the video and this is the best part with no watermark so we'll download a video and repurpose it and re-upload it onto Instagram

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is IG-first (inspo → film → edit → schedule via Publer). There is no documented cross-platform republishing automation from TikTok to IG. Publer does support multi-platform scheduling, but that requires manual upload to Publer — not automated downstream capture from TikTok. This bot fills a different gap: auto-capturing content the creator posts natively to TikTok.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real creators who self-post to TikTok independently of Palm's pipeline, evaluate tools like SaveTok, Repurpose.io, or similar that auto-detect new TikTok posts, strip watermarks, and push to IG Reels. This recovers content that otherwise bypasses Palm's pipeline and ensures it also benefits the IG funnel. Low effort, low risk — good quick win. Alternatively, enforce that all content goes through Palm's Publer pipeline first (avoiding the need for this bot entirely by controlling upload order).

findings.topic: Bot-based automation as an agency service/upsell tier
findings.title: Package automation bots as an upsell service to existing agency clients

variants.claim: Bundling bot services (story likes, comment likes, mass DM, reposter) as a premium add-on or inner-circle perk creates an additional revenue stream for the agency beyond standard revenue share.
variants.what_they_do: The agency offers these bot services to clients via a call-booking funnel (not self-serve). Existing Inner Circle / consulting clients get access for free as a retention perk. New clients book a qualification call before being granted access — controlling supply and adding perceived exclusivity. This creates a two-tier model: standard management + premium automation tier.

sources.quote: this is not something that we're just going to go ahead and launch to absolutely everybody... if you are already in Aura Consulting Pro in our Inner Circle in a Discord you already have access to this for free

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm operates on a revenue-share commission model (§7) with no documented upsell tiers or add-on services. Palm's current offering is full-service management. This competitor is carving out a productized automation layer that can be sold separately — potentially to creators Palm doesn't fully manage, or as an upsell to existing creators to accelerate their growth.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Explore whether Palm could offer a 'traffic acceleration' add-on (fixed monthly fee for IG bot traffic services) as a complement to the core rev-share deal. This would (a) add a predictable revenue stream, (b) give creators a concrete growth lever Palm controls, and (c) differentiate Palm's pitch vs. agencies offering only content + chat. Requires resolving the platform-risk SOP first. Flag for §7 pricing model discussion.

findings.title: Correct Snapchat profile configuration to avoid pre-ban flagging

variants.claim: Snapchat flags and bans accounts before they even start if the Bitmoji, public profile, bio, and Snapchat Plus subscription are not set up correctly.
variants.what_they_do: Agency follows a specific account setup checklist covering Bitmoji creation, public profile configuration, bio copy, and Snapchat Plus activation before doing any outreach or posting — treating setup as a trust-signal layer that precedes all activity.

sources.quote: The Bitmoji, the public profile, the bio, Snapchat Plus. If you get any of that wrong, Snapchat flags you before you even start

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Snapchat traffic strategy (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). Snapchat is not part of any current distribution playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building out Palm's traffic playbook, treat Snapchat account setup as a prerequisite checklist (Bitmoji, public profile, bio, Snapchat Plus) before any warm-up or posting begins — analogous to Palm's hardware-isolated warmup for AI personas (§3/§4) but applied to Snapchat accounts for real creators.

findings.title: Staged quick-add schedule to avoid same-day Snapchat ban

variants.claim: Snapchat bans accounts that add too many people too fast, so there is an exact daily add-limit schedule that must be followed and gradually increased.
variants.what_they_do: Agency uses a structured warm-up schedule with specific daily caps on how many users to quick-add, starting very low and incrementally increasing over time — no guessing on volume ramp.

sources.quote: There's an exact schedule for how many people to add at the start and when to increase it. If you just guess this part, you will be banned.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 90-day warmup cadence for AI social accounts (§4) with day-by-day activity rules, but this is IG/TikTok-focused. No equivalent Snapchat warm-up schedule exists. The principle (staged ramp, per-day caps) is directly analogous to Palm's existing warmup methodology.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Snapchat-specific warm-up schedule modeled on Palm's existing AI-account warmup structure (§4) — define Day 1–7 add limits, Day 8–14 increase thresholds, etc. The pattern Palm already uses for IG warmup translates directly; just need Snapchat-specific numbers researched and codified.

findings.topic: Snapchat Spotlight for organic fan acquisition
findings.title: Use Snapchat Spotlight posts strategically to drive inbound quick-adds

variants.claim: Posting correctly to Snapchat Spotlight generates organic views and causes target users to quick-add the creator, producing inbound traffic without manual outreach.
variants.what_they_do: Agency has a specific content format/posting method for Spotlight (not just spamming) that grows views and snap score simultaneously, converting Spotlight viewers into quick-adders who then enter the DM funnel.

sources.quote: Spotlight is where the organic traction comes from. But you can't just spam posts. There's a way to do it that gets you views and increases your snap score.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Snapchat presence or Spotlight strategy documented (§5 GAP). Palm's content engine (§1) is IG/TikTok Reels-focused. Spotlight is a parallel short-form organic channel that Palm is not utilizing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Investigate Spotlight as an organic top-of-funnel channel alongside IG Reels. Since Palm already has a data-driven content ideation pipeline (§1), the same inspo-scrape → analyze → film workflow could potentially surface Spotlight-optimized content formats. Prioritize learning what content format triggers Spotlight algorithmic distribution.

findings.topic: Snap score as social proof signal
findings.title: Grow snap score proactively to prevent target users from flagging account as fake

variants.claim: A low snap score causes potential fans who receive a quick-add to dismiss the account as a bot or fake, killing conversion before any content is seen.
variants.what_they_do: Agency actively builds snap score using a specific method before or during outreach so that when a man receives and checks the account, the score reads as a real, active user — functioning as social proof.

sources.quote: Snap score matters more than you think. It affects your visibility and your social proof. Cuz when a guy adds you back and he sees that your snap score is one 2 3 10 whatever, it seems slow. He thinks you're a fake account.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup (§4) explicitly addresses social proof (posting cadence, stories, engagement activity during warmup before monetization). The same logic applies here to Snapchat snap score. Palm has no Snapchat equivalent of this.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add snap score cultivation as a mandatory warmup metric in any future Snapchat playbook — analogous to how Palm's IG warmup tracks follower growth and engagement rate before enabling OF CTAs. Define a minimum snap score threshold before beginning active quick-add outreach.

findings.title: Route Snapchat traffic through Telegram as a conversion bridge to OnlyFans

variants.claim: Snapchat traffic must be funneled through a specifically configured Telegram step rather than linking directly to OnlyFans, because direct link-spamming gets accounts banned and reduces conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up a Telegram channel/bot as a middle layer between Snapchat stories/DMs and the OnlyFans link — the Snapchat account drives viewers to Telegram, and Telegram is configured to convert them into OF subscribers. Direct OF links in Snapchat are avoided.

sources.quote: This is how you actually convert the traffic you're getting. Without it, you're just posting stories for no reason. There's a specific way to set up Telegram so people actually click through and end up subbing to your only fans.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses Telegram operationally (Amin contractor coordination, posting webhooks — §5), but not as a consumer-facing traffic funnel. Palm's current funnel is social → OF directly (§0). This Snapchat → Telegram → OF bridge is a distinct multi-hop architecture Palm has not documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building a Snapchat traffic playbook, adopt the Telegram bridge model: Snapchat stories/DMs → Telegram CTA → OF subscribe. Document the Telegram channel setup (e.g., pinned OF link, welcome message, drip content) as a reusable module. This also reduces platform risk of OF link detection on Snapchat.

findings.topic: Reddit content moderation / NSFW risk management
findings.title: Default to SFW Reddit content; if NSFW, limit to topless — protects accounts and creator comfort

variants.claim: Keeping Reddit content SFW or limited to topless-only reduces platform risk and creator friction while still driving meaningful OF traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends SFW content as the default Reddit strategy. If the creator is comfortable with more explicit material, caps NSFW at topless (boobs). Explicitly does not recommend full explicit content on Reddit unless the creator has abundant exclusive vault content and is fully comfortable. Applies this rule to both real and AI model accounts.

sources.quote: I would personally stick to SFW. If you're going to do some NSFW, even whether it's for real or AI models, I'd mainly only recommend them boobs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is documented as thin on OF vault/PPV content strategy (§2) and has no Reddit traffic SOP (§5 GAP). Palm already produces SFW social content for IG (§1) — this recommendation aligns with that existing posture and extends it cleanly to Reddit.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 'SFW-first, topless maximum' as Palm's default Reddit content policy in the Reddit SOP. This protects real creator comfort and account longevity. For creators willing to go further, create a tiered content permission checklist (SFW / topless / explicit) that is agreed at onboarding — feeding into the thin creator onboarding documentation (§6).

findings.title: Six-step ad script for qualified creator leads

variants.claim: A repeatable six-step ad script (attention, key benefit, without-the-pain, proof, time-sensitive offer, downside of inaction) structures creative for conversion rather than ad-hoc messaging.
variants.what_they_do: Uses a fixed 6-step structure with one chosen angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease); language must be coherent from ad to VSL to funnel to call and matched to a specific avatar (e.g. creators already doing 10k/mo) to avoid attracting low-quality opportunity-seekers.

sources.quote: the six-step ad script looks like this... number one, attention... number six is the downside of inaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No acquisition messaging framework or avatar definition exists at Palm (§6 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 6-step script and define a concrete creator avatar (revenue band, follower count) so Palm outreach and any future content speaks one coherent, qualifying message.

findings.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.

findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Confirms Palm's core OnlyFans funnel as the high-conversion asset (§0). Palm's AI personas use OnlyFans-via-social, so this validates Palm's choice not to go Fanvue-only.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold the OnlyFans funnel as the conversion anchor for both real and AI lines; treat Fanvue-style platforms only as fallback, never primary.

findings.title: 20% real / 80% AI mix to keep trust and volume

variants.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
variants.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.

sources.quote: 20% real content kept people trusting it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature data-driven social pipeline (§1) but no codified real-vs-AI blend ratio for a hybrid creator. This gives a concrete starting ratio.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default ~20-30% real / 70-80% AI blend for hybrid creators, front-loading real content during launch; codify the ratio in the SMM content spec.

findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

findings.title: Motion control lets you copy trends in minutes

variants.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
variants.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.

sources.quote: see a trend, generate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies WHAT to film from viral data but still depends on the creator filming. Motion control collapses the produce-time gap Palm currently has.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a motion-control fast-path to the inspo pipeline so promoted trend reels can be AI-rendered for hybrid creators within the trend window.

findings.title: Engagement-trap, trendjack, and paid promo pages on X

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

sources.quote: the content designed to get replies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is undocumented (§5 gap). These are specific paid + organic X tactics with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture as build-out menu for an X traffic SOP: engagement-trap caption library, daily trend monitoring, and a tested promo-page budget per creator.

findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

findings.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

findings.title: Bans driven by unbranded sexual content, not IP/proxies

variants.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
variants.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.

sources.quote: 90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is hardware-isolated with proxies/device separation (§3/§4); for REAL creators Palm has no documented IG ban-avoidance posture (§4 gap). This reframes the real-creator risk as content-led.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real-creator IG, prioritize branded non-explicit content and normal-user behavior over technical spoofing; document a real-creator ban-avoidance SOP.

findings.title: Branded persona replacing pure volume in 2026

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline optimizes for viral content ideation (§1) but persona/branding is not a codified input. This argues brand should be a first-class field upstream of content selection.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a defined persona/brand brief to creator onboarding and feed it into the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas reinforce a consistent identity.

findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

findings.title: Snapchat as an under-saturated OF traffic + income channel

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is IG/TikTok via Publer + Telegram (§5); Snapchat is absent from Palm's stack. New channel with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot Snapchat for one high-output creator: repurpose existing content, weekly-only CTA, test promoted snaps; measure as a third revenue lever.

findings.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.

findings.title: Keep the profile wall teasing, never explicit, to preserve PPV value

variants.claim: Profile/wall content should tease (lingerie, not nudity) so curiosity—and willingness to pay for PPV—stays intact.
variants.what_they_do: Refuses to put nudes or explicit content on the OF wall, reasoning that if the fan sees everything for free or at subscription price there is nothing left to sell; keeps the wall at lingerie-level to sustain curiosity.

sources.quote: do not put any nudes... why is the fan going to spend any money when he's basically seen all of that for free

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social top-of-funnel content (§1 MATURE) but thin on OF-vault/PPV content strategy (§2 THIN); this is a concrete vault-merchandising rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a 'tease-not-reveal on the wall' rule as part of Palm's OF-vault/PPV content guidance.

findings.title: Win back expired subscribers with a discounted mass message on day one

variants.claim: Immediately re-activate a new creator's expired/lapsed subscribers with a discounted or trial-link mass message before chasing new traffic.
variants.what_they_do: On signing a creator, mass-message her expired subs (often already in the following list via auto-follow) with a trial/discount link; expect a spike of returning subs in the first week. Framed as the easy first win because retaining/reactivating is cheaper than finding new viral reels.

sources.quote: send out a mass message to all of her existing expired subscribers... put together a trial link or a discount link... you should see a big spike of these expired subs coming back.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented win-back/reactivation routine (§9 GAP); chatting outsourced (§8), so this is build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: make a day-one expired-subs win-back blast a standard onboarding step in the in-house/AI chat workflow.

findings.title: Manage creator expectations week-by-week or they invent their own

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented expectation-setting framework in onboarding (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bake a week-1/month-1/month-2 expectation script into the onboarding call to reduce early churn.

findings.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

findings.title: Provision reserve accounts for burn

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: 100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI account warmup (§3/§4 MATURE) but has no documented reserve-account or burn-rate provisioning model. Gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a reserve-account buffer per AI persona plus an automated daily ban-detection check, sized to an expected burn rate, so a ban never stalls a creator's funnel.

findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

findings.title: Give every model a written character

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline drives content from viral inspo data (§1) but there is no documented per-creator character/persona layer tying content to a sales story. Gap on branding/type-2.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a per-creator character sheet (persona, backstory, target fan demographic) that both content and the chat team work from, so content ladders into monetization.

findings.title: Ladder PPV prices upward in sequence

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: No formal price ladder at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Publish a standard Palm PPV ladder with rising steps and trigger conditions, instead of single high asks.
